speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 19, 1996. The survivor is Cela Sarna, maiden name is Nisenbaum. I'm the interviewer, Ruth Meyer. The interview is being conducted in Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey, in the United States. And the interview will be conducted in English. Hi. I'm Ruth Meyer. Today is June 19 1996. We are with Cela Sarna in Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey. Hi. Let's start by telling us your name, please, and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is Cela Sarna-- C-E-L-A. Sarna is S-A-R-N-A. And I was born in Miedzyrzecz, Poland. Should I spell it, Miedzyrzecz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-- M-I-E-N-D-Z-Y-R-Z-E-C, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? Your maiden name, and spell that too.

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Cyna, C-Y-N-A, Nisenbaum-- N-I-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1924, the 22nd of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now that makes you how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't count it out yet. [LAUGHTER] I think 73 or 74.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: 73, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's start by telling me about your family-- your parents' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was Freda, F-R-E-D-A, Feldbaum, F-E-L-D-B-A-U-M. And I don't know her date of birth. And my-- my father was Moyshe-- M-R-- M-O-Y-S-H-E-- Nisenbaum. You had spelled it already. And we were all in [? Miedzyrzecz. ?] We were all in Miedzyrzecz. And I had-- that's good. I had seven sisters and one brother. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, give me a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought I would be better than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're fine. Tell-- tell me your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your sisters' and brothers' names. If you can give me who-- starting with the oldest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --if you know how much older they were than you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were much older, because I-- my father was in America for four or five years. He only had two children. And he made some money and sent it home, and we built a beautiful [? tree ?] family house. And we all lived-- when I was born, I had already three sisters married and a brother. So I don't remember their births or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So give me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Esther, and gee-- and Leah and Breindel, and then was my brother, Yankel. And then was my sister, Gitel and then Itkie, and Peskie, and I. Two of them are alive. They were in Russia. When the war broke out, first came the Russian to our town. And they were maybe two weeks. And then they called us all. We didn't have any radios or anything. So they called us all into the middle of the city. And they told us that they are going away, that the border between Russia and Germany will be Brest-Litovsk. That's Brest [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And we should all go with them, because the Germans will come in, and they'll kill us all out. But we didn't believe them. We all were in the house. The war just broke out. It was cozy. We were all together. All my sisters had three, four children. One had five. And where will you go? We didn't have a lot of riches. But whatever had-- we had was a treasure to my mother. She lived in this house for so many years. Where will she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my two sisters went with the Russians. They packed their belongings. One was married, didn't have any children yet. And one was not married yet. And they both went. They wanted to take me along. But my mother didn't trust me with them. And I was left at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back, please, to your early childhood, before the war broke out. Tell me about your home. You said that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --father had gone to America and sent money home to build this house. Can you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This house?

speaker: interviewer
text: The house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lived in three rooms and a kitchen, which it was very, very lavish in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me. And my father worked with my brother together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were making [? brushes. ?] This was actually-- the-- the whole town did this. And it was not bad. We made a living. Of course, whatever was living there is very poor here. And my mother sent me to a private school, a folk school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a Jewish school, a private.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started out-- I have pictures on that. When I started out, I was five. I went to the school until I was eight. The reason she took me out, simple-- she couldn't afford to pay for me. I hated the first of the month. Every month, they-- they used to send me home for money. And finally, I-- when I got a little older, I said, I don't want to go to this school. I don't want to be sent home every-- every month for money. I want to go to a public school. And she took me out. And she sent me to a public school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about the house you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a lot of beds [LAUGHS] because we are-- we were a lot of children in one room, and then a kitchen in a big-- they called this a saloon. It was a big [INAUDIBLE], very nice. I think my father send this from America. I'm not sure, because it was very pretty. And we had a nice dining room table. Oh, and-- that's it. This was in the-- in the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there gas, electricity, indoor plumbing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had-- we had no electricity, no indoor plumbing, no toilets. There was one toilet for maybe 10 families, where they lived all in one on this place. And we all went-- what can I say?-- there to this toilet. At night, if you needed, my mother had to go with me, because I was afraid to go out. It was dark. And that's how-- we were a middle-- let's say not poor, not rich, but comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of people were your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very lovable. First of all, I was the youngest. So they-- all the sisters loved me, because they already had children about my age-- very good parents. If my-- I had girlfriends, where they were much richer than I am. And then we-- Saturday, we wanted to go to see a movie, Shirley Temple or whatever it was. It was $0.50, which it was a lot of money. She didn't have it. But she never let them go out from the house until she borrowed it from the next door neighbor-- I should go to the movie with them. She was very, very lovable. Very nice parents I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Shabbat in your house and the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the holidays and Shabbat, everybody was religious. It wasn't such a thing that somebody didn't have any [? cows ?] or anything. So everybody stayed home. We used to make a cholent Friday and send it over to the baker. And-- and Saturday, noontime, we used to take it out from the baker. My mother used to pay for it Friday. And then we needed tea. So we went to one place a little bit further. I mean, this was everything on the same street. We lived on the nicest street in this town. I used to go. I used to go bring tea-- not the tea, just the water. She cooked it. And we paid ahead of time. And then we bring-- we brought [INAUDIBLE]. And we had tea with the cholent, with some meat. One day, she sent me for the cholent. I'll tell you a-- a funny story. And old cholent looked the same way, because they were-- my mother already recognized her-- her thing, what she put it into. And I brought home, and my mother looked at it, and she said, oh, my god. That's not mine. And she took it fast back and brought ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in cholent?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was some meat and potatoes, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family go on vacations during the summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a time that your whole family was living together? Where did your married sisters live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in the same town. But they got married. They lived in another town. But when they got married, my mother had-- I told you, was a [? tree ?] family. And since she had a lot of children, so she allowed them for three years to live in our house. And when another one's supposed to get married, they moved on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the weddings were like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually was just at one wedding, because by the-- yeah. And it was-- yeah, it was a small wedding, made in the house. We all-- it wasn't like here, but it was a nice wedding. Klezmers were playing. And-- and we-- they danced all night. This was the only sister was married what I remember. And there was another wedding, when she married in 1939. And already, Hitler started coming, [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't come to Poland yet. But they send out all the Germans from-- if they had families-- and the war already was boiling. So she got married. But it was a very private wedding, in-- in the house, with just their family and our family and the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me earlier about changing schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the public school was a public school. I went into the second grade right away because I already learned in this school. And I went every day to school. And I came back 4 o'clock. And on the way back, I used to go to learn Hebrew. Well, this wasn't a lot of money. I think it was one zloty for all week. And I used-- when I came in on Friday, I brought the zloty. I didn't like the school, really. And I didn't want to go. So one day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was a-- it was not a cheder, because a cheder, they called the-- for the boys. It was for the girls. And I want to just say it, and it just doesn't come to me. All right, maybe later, it will come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, to this school, I went. And I really didn't like it. And I didn't want to go. Well, it's going to be hard to explain it now. One day, she sat me on her knee, and she said to me-- I said, I won't go tomorrow. She said to me, you know what? I'll tell you something. I'm an older woman, and some day I'll die. I said and-- she said, if you don't say [YIDDISH]. I won't be able to go to heaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said it in Yiddish. And-- and if you-- and if you won't go to the school, you won't learn how to say [YIDDISH]. And with that-- this thought, I went every time we had to go, and I learned very well. I still want to remember the school, and I can't remember. Anyway, this was-- so I went to a public school. And I went to this school to learn Hebrew. And I came home. At night, we had supper. It wasn't a lot of children then at home, because just my sisters-- the ones where they lived through the war and me, because the others were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your friends at school. Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had only Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there non-Jews at the school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, more non-Jews than Jews, because this was a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your relationship with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, was fine, but they lived in different parts of the town, and I never saw them. But on Saturday, all the Jews got together. All my friends are Jewish. We got together, went to the movies. We belonged to a Zionist organization. So we went dancing, [INAUDIBLE], and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you remember any anti-Semitic incidents in school or as a young child before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in our town was 90-- 80% Jews. So really-- I hear a lot of people talking, but really, I-- in our place where we lived, I never saw anything or heard anything, really not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- when did you start to feel that there were troubles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, when-- really, before-- a couple months before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mmhm. Do you remember anything about observances, Pesach, in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very well observed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the preparations for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, two weeks before, we started making matzah. We-- it was in private homes. There were people working. And they were preparing the matzah and baking them. And my mother used to go in and buy whatever she needed. I think they bought it in a big-- I don't know how many pounds, really. But they brought it home. And we had it over the Pesach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do in the house before Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she cleaned. The whole the house was empty. They took out the beds. And they-- and they cleaned the beds, and they washed the floors. And they-- they painted the walls with white and took out all the [YIDDISH], because we had a lot. And our whole [INAUDIBLE] was outside. It was a real preparation. I hated this. Before really came the holiday, she was so tired that she couldn't wait until it's over. It was a big preparation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have the Seder at your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had then the Seder. All the kids came to our house. And we all-- always had my father with all the children, was sitting ahead of the table, and we had the Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest. I always I am ashamed to say it, but I never was till the end. I always fell asleep. My mother used to put me to bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember saying the four questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- we had younger kids. My sisters' younger children, they were saying the four questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what started happening that you knew, your family knew, that there was trouble starting for the Jews in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It really came out in one time. The war came out, and it's-- from day to day, we had different things. The first thing was-- well, we felt it right away, because in a month, the Germans took over the whole street, because, I said, it was the nicest street. And they threw us out. They gave us only three hours to pack the bags and go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '39, about December. It was very cold. And my-- the older brothers and the brother and all their brother-in-laws came. They helped us pack, because they lived in a different-- a different part of the town. And they-- they were not relocated. And we packed whatever we could. And they found us a place. It was horrible and it-- on the second floor, a walk up, with a little kitchen and just one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take your furniture and belongings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No furniture. No, we left everything. Just the beds we took. And-- because it-- this place where we went to was a place just to put two beds, really. And we moved there. And-- and from then on, we already started feeling the war, because [INAUDIBLE]-- when you are not in your own home, you already, you are a refugee right way. And the place where we moved was by the big shul. They converted the shul to a displaced person place. And they started bringing people from the smaller towns into our town. And they located them in this big shul. We had a beautiful shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the shul that you and your family went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we really went to a shtiebel, where it was-- it was smaller. It wasn't a [INAUDIBLE]. It was a nice synagogue. But that was on the same street where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made-- they took all the homes, and they made it for a commandant too for the Germans-- not only my house, it was hundreds of houses. 100 houses on this place, on this street. The whole street, nobody could go in. I-- later, we went in, because we went every day to-- to clean for the Germans. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did get to see your house again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never went as far as my-- my house, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about the shtiebel. Do you remember the rabbi's name or anything about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the rabbi's name. But my mother used to go with me Saturdays, used to take me to the-- to the synagogue. My mother was well educated in Yiddish and in Polish. And-- and it was very seldom in Europe that the people were educated in that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother used to sit in the middle. You know, now, I remember the thing. In Bais Yaakov, I went. Now, it came. So she sat in the middle with the [YIDDISH], they called it in Yiddish. And all the women were sitting around. And I sat on her knees.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was davening. An older woman was sitting here crying, and I hated it. I really didn't want to see my mother crying and all the other women crying. But she took me for a while until I grow up a little older, and I didn't want to go anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they were crying? Did you ever ask her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because if you know what you're saying, you pray to God, they should be healthy, they should marry all of their children and have some money, you know-- and people were very religious years ago, not religious what it was good for them, but really religious. They fanatic. They believed in it-- what they were saying, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were moved out of your house. And you went to live in this small apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was already in. My mother used to go every day-- not every day, every second day-- to stay half-- a half a night in the-- in a line for a piece of bread. Right away, we didn't have nothing to eat, because we had only the rations, what they gave us. It's a funny part that we-- you ask me about anti-Semite. We couldn't walk on the sidewalk. Right away, we had yellow stars in a yellow band on the-- on the arm, on the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1939 already?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right away in the end of 1939. And when we had the rations, we didn't have enough to eat. My mother used to stay in line. And then I figured, why should she stay in the line? I'll go. And I used to stay every day in the line. [? Not ?] once, I stayed a half a night, and when it came to me, they said that they don't have any more bread. In 19-- the beginning of '40, I think it was like in January-- it was on a Tuesday. I stayed in the line and I came in. And my mother was in bed. And all of a sudden, she woke up, and she says, oh, I forgot to [YIDDISH]. I said, no, today is Tuesday. And I realized right away that she caught the typhus fever, because this was going around. People were dying like the flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the synagogue, what I said, from this shul, they used to take out 20 a day, 30 a day, because this is very, very contagious. If once has it-- one has it in the family, the whole family gets it. What should I do? So I ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing I'll tell you, before I start out-- when the war out-- was out, I was a child, but I grew up in one year, I think 30 years. I knew how to take care of myself. I knew how to be wise. I knew when-- when to do anything. I grew up very, very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, that time, already, I think, 14. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, I went to the doctor, and the doctor came up. He knew us very well. He was a neighbor of ours when we lived on this-- yet in our own-- own street. He came up, and he said, she has typhus fever. He said, the best thing, to take her to the hospital. That time was yet a Jewish hospital. We took her to the hospital. And typhus fever has like a-- a date-- seven days later. You-- oh, you turned better, or you die. It's like a crisis. If you live through the crisis, you live. Anyway, she lived through the crisis. And we took her home. Yeah. I forgot to say that my father had typhus fever in the First World War. So this, you can have only once in your life. And he was the one-- he took her to the hospital on his back in his-- and he really [? worked ?] [? around with ?] all of us. When she was in the hospital, I got it with my sister. We went through. But we already were in the house, because he-- he saw that they-- they don't take care of, really, of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he didn't trust them. And he took care of us. And we were sick at home. And my mother came home after two weeks. She was very weak. Do you remember approximately how old she was at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was 60. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And-- and we lived through this. And then in 19-- well, they took us to work every day. And really, they-- they took-- my father shaved off the beard. Otherwise, the Germans shaved it off if they saw it. So they shaved it off with-- with flesh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did it himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did it himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you weren't going to school anymore after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Right away, school started-- well, I graduated, really, school then, because when we were 13, we graduated school-- grammar school. We went seven years. And we had to go to work. My father was older than my mother yet. So we didn't want the father was-- to go to school-- to work. So I really worked every day. One day, I went with my [GERMAN]. And one day I went with his [GERMAN]. And he stayed home. He stayed home, and I used to clean by the Germans. I used to wash the floors. And that's what we did. We didn't clean them. We washed the floors. This was my occupation. Or we-- we went to a-- to a kitchen where they cooked for the Germans, and we peeled potatoes, carrots, prepared everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sisters go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- no. Only me and my other sister, when she was still not married and she stayed with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So you worked there for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: For all the time until I got relocated.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. I think we'll stop now and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]
